prompt: 2026: When an enterprise security team reviews an AI VENDOR (a startup that ships an AI agent/LLM feature), what EXACT evidence do they now require for the AI portion of the review (cite real questionnaires, frameworks, buyer posts, RFPs from 2025-2026)? Is anyone delivering a buyer-VERIFIABLE signed AI audit artifact (not a dashboard, not runtime defense) that the buyer checks independently? Who is closest and what is the unmet gap? Also: what makes an automated, log-based AI audit CREDIBLE to a skeptical enterprise reviewer vs dismissed as a self-attestation, and what do reviewers say they distrust about automated/AI-generated security evidence? Quote real reviewers/buyers/CISOs.
content:
**Enterprise security teams in 2026 reviewing AI vendors (especially startups with LLM/agent features) require AI-specific supplements to standard security questionnaires (e.g., SIG, CAIQ, or SOC 2 attestations). These focus on model governance, data handling for AI, bias/fairness, transparency/explainability, prompt injection/red-teaming, output controls, auditability of decisions, and regulatory readiness (e.g., EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001).**[[1]](https://assets.aclu.org/live/uploads/2025/06/ACLU-Vendor-Generative-AI-General-Questionnaire-Public-Facing-Copy.pdf)[[2]](https://worqlo.com/blog/enterprise-ai-vendor-rfp-questions/)

Real examples from 2025–2026 include:

- **ACLU Generative AI Vendor Questionnaire** (public version updated June 2025): This supplements general security/privacy reviews for any GenAI that cannot be fully disabled. It asks about policies/practices on security, privacy, copyright, bias/fairness, transparency, and related issues. It is intended for procurement and explicitly notes it is *not* comprehensive but targets GenAI-specific risks.[[1]](https://assets.aclu.org/live/uploads/2025/06/ACLU-Vendor-Generative-AI-General-Questionnaire-Public-Facing-Copy.pdf)

- **Enterprise AI Vendor RFP frameworks** (e.g., worqlo 2026 guide with 40 questions across 7 categories): These include Data Security & Privacy (e.g., exact data processing locations, encryption, retention), model governance/training data sourcing, evaluation/red-teaming, harmful output mitigation, update policies, auditability/explainability, and compliance statements. Standard IT RFPs are noted as missing ~60% of AI-relevant risks.[[2]](https://worqlo.com/blog/enterprise-ai-vendor-rfp-questions/)

- Other vendor questionnaires (e.g., optro.ai, atlassystems 2026 templates) emphasize evidence on data handling, model governance, security protocols, misuse prevention, and control validation.[[3]](https://optro.ai/blog/ai-vendor-questionnaire-essential-questions-to-ask)[[4]](https://www.atlassystems.com/blog/ai-vendor-risk-questionnaire)

- Broader requirements tie into frameworks like EU AI Act (high-risk transparency/audit obligations effective 2026), NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, and sector rules (SOX, HIPAA, etc.), often demanding minimum audit trail schemas (e.g., 12 fields: NTP-timestamp, decision ID, user/AI identities/versions, inputs with attribution, invoked policy, reasoning, output, action, approvals, tamper-evident proof).[[5]](https://www.kognitos.com/blog/ai-audit-trail-requirements-2026-checklist/)

Reviews also commonly request model cards, evaluation reports, red-team results, acceptable use/incident response policies, and evidence of testing for prompt injection, data leakage, or bias.[[6]](https://www.promptfoo.dev/blog/ai-regulation-2025/)

**Buyer-verifiable signed AI audit artifacts (independent of dashboards or runtime defenses) exist but are emerging/not yet standard.** Examples of closest offerings include:

- **Proxara Compliance-Grade AI Audit**: Generates per-interaction evidence mapped to rules (e.g., SEC Reg S-P, FINRA, EU AI Act Arts. 12/26), with an immutable trail anchored to a public transparency log.[[7]](https://proxara.ai/compliance-grade-audit)
- **ai-audit-trail (Python library, v0.4.7+ May 2026)**: Produces Ed25519-signed, hash-chained Decision Receipts with mappings to ISO 42001/NIST AI RMF/EU AI Act; designed for verifiable compliance packages.[[8]](https://pypi.org/project/ai-audit-trail/0.4.7/)
- **Forge Certified AI Audit**: Ed25519-signed, SHA-512 hash-chained behavioral assessments (base models or deployments) with independent verification links and EU AI Act mapping.[[9]](https://www.forge-nc.dev/enterprise)
- **Evidary**: Cryptographically signed evidence bundles/packages for reviewer inspection (local verification without API keys).[[10]](https://www.evidary.com/)
- Related: VAOL (Verifiable AI Output Ledger) on GitHub for tamper-evident signed LLM output ledgers using DSSE/Merkle proofs.[[11]](https://github.com/ogulcanaydogan/Verifiable-AI-Output-Ledger)

These produce buyer-checkable cryptographic artifacts (signatures + chains) rather than vendor-controlled views. **Who is closest**: The crypto-signed ledger/artifact providers above (especially those with public anchoring or open verification). **Unmet gap**: Standardization/adoption in mainstream TPRM/RFP processes; most buyers still default to questionnaires + SOC 2/ISO attestations. Independent third-party verification tools for these artifacts are limited, and integration with enterprise GRC/TPRM platforms lags. Widespread skepticism of vendor-generated evidence persists, so these remain niche (often in regulated sectors like finance/healthtech).[[12]](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/trust-control-drop-in-ai-audit-thomas-fox-rh1ac)

**Automated/log-based AI audits gain credibility through cryptographic protections (Ed25519 signatures, hash-chaining/tamper-evidence), independent/public verifiability, explicit regulatory mappings, and reconstruction of decisions (inputs, reasoning, policy invoked, outputs) rather than summaries.** They are dismissed as self-attestation when the generating system also controls/writes the logs (structural gap), lacks third-party or crypto proof, or relies on opaque vendor processes. Reviewers distrust AI-generated or automated evidence due to accountability/transparency concerns, potential bias in the evidence itself, and the vendor's ability to curate or alter records.[[13]](https://www.a-lign.com/?p=gtaqnadgjbc)[[14]](https://www.pivotpointsecurity.com/the-ai-governance-trap-when-your-ai-guru-becomes-judge-jury-and-executioner/)

Real reviewer/CISO-adjacent commentary includes: "That is not governance. It is self-attestation. This approach fails every meaningful framework test" (on internal AI governance producing its own records). "The gap is structural: the application that produces the decision also writes the record, which is the self-attestation problem." CISOs increasingly reject point-in-time/annual self-attestations in favor of continuous, independently verifiable evidence; surveys show ~53% of compliance pros have concerns about AI in audits specifically around accountability and transparency.[[14]](https://www.pivotpointsecurity.com/the-ai-governance-trap-when-your-ai-guru-becomes-judge-jury-and-executioner/)[[15]](https://www.deepinspect.ai/blog/state-of-ai-compliance-q2-2026-mini-report)[[13]](https://www.a-lign.com/?p=gtaqnadgjbc)

In short, 2026 reviews demand AI-specific questionnaire/RFP evidence plus verifiable audit trails; crypto-signed artifacts are the direction for buyer-independent checks, but self-attestation distrust remains a core barrier until these become standardized and easily verifiable at scale.
